planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container-grown plants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May or September–October. Purple Heart is a tender perennial in zone 7–9 that may die back to the ground in hard freezes but resprouts reliably from the roots in spring. The trailing, spreading stems root wherever nodes contact moist soil, rapidly covering ground in sunny to partially shaded areas. Stem cuttings root extremely easily — simply cut a stem and place it in moist soil or water, where roots develop within 1–2 weeks. The vivid purple color intensifies in full sun and fades somewhat in shade; in very deep shade the foliage may appear more green-purple than the vivid, saturated purple of full-sun plants.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Purple Heart is drought tolerant once established and blooms most freely with the most vivid purple foliage in full sun, while tolerating partial shade; Purple Heart (Tradescantia pallida, formerly Setcreasea pallida) is one of the most striking purple-foliaged plants available for zone 9 gardens, producing a intense, saturated, clear purple color on both the trailing stems and the large (4–5 inch), lance-shaped leaves that is unique among commonly grown ground covers and border plants. Small, three-petaled, bright pink-magenta flowers appear at the stem tips in summer, providing a complementary floral dimension to the primary foliage display. The trailing, ground-covering habit (stems rooting where they contact soil) makes Purple Heart one of the most effective and low-maintenance purple ground covers available — spreading steadily without becoming invasive in most garden contexts, rooting easily from stem pieces, and recovering quickly from damage or drought when conditions improve. In zone 9 Purple Heart is at its best — thriving in the summer heat that stresses many ornamentals and maintaining its vivid color through the entire growing season from spring through fall. The vivid purple provides exceptional contrast with chartreuse, silver-gray, and gold-foliaged companion plants.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tradescantia pallida for Texas Landscapes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Purple Heart as Florida Ground Cover
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Purple-Foliaged Plants for Gulf South Gardens

planting.note: Place outdoors in shade after temperatures remain above 55°F; in zone 9 use outdoors April–October and bring indoors in winter. Pink Quill Tillandsia is an epiphytic bromeliad — it can be grown mounted on driftwood, in a shallow container with fast-draining orchid bark mix, or attached to a tree branch in zone 10–11. Absorbs water and nutrients through the leaf scales (trichomes); mist or soak the plant 2–3 times weekly in warm weather, ensuring it dries fully between mistings. The spectacular pink inflorescence — a flat, paddle-shaped, bright rose-pink quill — is the primary ornamental feature, lasting 2–3 months before the blue-violet flowers emerge from its edges and then fade. After flowering, the plant produces offsets (pups) from the base that can be separated when they reach half the parent size.

directions: Plant in bright indirect light to deep shade — never in direct sun, which bleaches and damages the foliage — mounted on driftwood or in a container with very fast-draining orchid bark; water by misting or soaking the entire plant; Tillandsia cyanea (Pink Quill Plant) is an epiphytic bromeliad from Ecuador that produces one of the most visually dramatic floral structures available in any commonly grown houseplant or shade-tolerant ornamental — a striking, large, flat, paddle-shaped, bright hot-pink to rose inflorescence (the 'quill') up to 6 inches tall from which 1-inch violet-blue flowers sequentially emerge along both edges over a period of weeks. The color combination of the hot-pink bracts and violet flowers is exceptional and entirely natural — creating a bicolor display that would seem improbably vivid if it were not a real, wild-type species rather than a hybridized selection. As an epiphyte, Pink Quill Tillandsia requires no soil — it grows naturally attached to tree branches, absorbing water and nutrients through the specialized leaf trichomes (scales) that cover the gray-green leaves. For zone 9 gardeners with covered porches, pergolas, and outdoor living spaces that receive no direct sun, Pink Quill Tillandsia offers one of the most spectacular and conversation-provoking shade ornamental plants available, with minimal care requirements beyond regular misting.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tillandsia Species for Florida Landscapes and Interiors
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Bromeliads for Gulf South Shade Gardens
American Bromeliad Society — Tillandsia cyanea Cultivation Guide

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Red Alternanthera is used extensively in zone 9 as a colorful, low-growing bedding and border-edging plant — often planted in mass to create geometric patterns (carpet bedding) in formal garden designs. Shear plants lightly every 4–6 weeks to maintain compact, dense form and prevent legginess; unpruned plants become open and produce fewer of the intensely colored leaves. The color is most vivid in full sun; partial shade produces larger, slightly less vivid leaves. In zone 9 it performs as a warm-season annual (tender below about 35°F), though established plants may resprout from stems in mild winters.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil — Red Alternanthera produces the most vivid leaf color in full sun but tolerates partial shade and is often used in partially shaded border situations; Red Alternanthera (Alternanthera ficoidea 'Red') is a low-growing, densely leafed foliage annual producing small, narrow leaves in vivid shades of red, burgundy, and bronze-red — creating a solid color mass of warm, intensely pigmented foliage that is used in traditional carpet bedding, border edging, and colorful pattern planting. Unlike the more rounded, dark-red-leaved Alternanthera dentata varieties, A. ficoidea has smaller, narrower leaves and a lower, denser growth habit, making it particularly useful for tight geometric patterns and fine-textured edging where a compact, fine-textured plant is needed. The richly colored leaves provide the same seasonal color function as flowering annuals but with purely foliage-based appeal — valuable in designs where consistent, uniform color without the maintenance of deadheading or the variability of flower timing is preferred. Red Alternanthera thrives in zone 9's summer heat and humidity, which would stress many cool-season foliage plants, growing most vigorously and producing its most intensely colored leaves in the hottest months of the year.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Alternanthera ficoidea for Florida Landscapes
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Annual Foliage Plants for Gulf South Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Warm-Season Color Annuals for Zone 9

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–April. Oroblanco is a grapefruit-pomelo hybrid developed by UC Riverside, released in 1981 — less cold-hardy than grapefruit or satsuma, tolerating temperatures only to about 27–28°F. In zone 9a plant in the most protected microclimate (south or west wall exposure) and protect young trees with frost cloth during the first 3–4 winters. Harvest in winter (December–February) when the thick, pale yellow-green rind begins to yellow — the fruit does not need to fully color for the flesh to develop full sweetness. The flesh is notably sweeter than standard grapefruit, with lower acidity and bitterness.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil with consistent deep watering during the growing season — Oroblanco citrus requires full sun, good drainage, and regular irrigation for fruit production; Oroblanco is a grapefruit-pomelo hybrid (Citrus x paradisi) developed at UC Riverside specifically to combine the large, sweet, low-acid flesh of pomelo with the familiar grapefruit flavor profile — producing a fruit that is distinctly sweeter and less bitter than standard white or pink grapefruit while retaining the clean, refreshing citrus character that makes grapefruit family fruits distinctive. The large, pale yellow-green to yellow fruit (5–6 inches in diameter) has a thick rind and abundant, juicy, very pale yellow-white flesh with the reduced bitterness that makes Oroblanco accessible to consumers who find standard grapefruit too bitter without sugar. Oroblanco is one of the most productive and consistent hybrid citrus varieties for zone 9 — the combination of large fruit size, high juice content, and notably superior eating quality compared to bitter grapefruit types makes it a practical and rewarding home citrus tree in the warm, protected microclimates of zone 9 where adequate winter temperatures allow year-round outdoor culture.
sources: University of California Riverside — Oroblanco Citrus Variety Development
University of California Cooperative Extension — Oroblanco Grapefruit-Pomelo Hybrid
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Sweet Grapefruit Varieties for Gulf Coast

planting.note: Direct sow in full sun after last frost when soil is warm; in zone 9 sow March–June for summer bloom or August–September for fall bloom. Profusion White, like all Profusion series zinnias, is a fully interspecific hybrid zinnia (Z. elegans x Z. angustifolia) that produces single to semi-double flowers with superior disease resistance over standard Z. elegans varieties — the interspecific parentage provides natural resistance to powdery mildew that is a serious problem for Z. elegans in humid Gulf South summers. The medium-sized (2–3 inch), pure white flowers bloom continuously without deadheading. Profusion White won an All-America Selections award — the AAS Flower Award — specifically for its combination of heat tolerance, disease resistance, and continuous flowering in summer trial gardens across the US.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Profusion White zinnia thrives in hot, sunny conditions and blooms most prolifically with minimal water stress; Profusion White is an All-America Selections award-winning interspecific zinnia hybrid producing a spreading, mounding plant covered with clean white, single to semi-double flowers (2–3 inches) from early summer through frost. The interspecific hybrid origin (Zinnia elegans x Z. angustifolia) gives Profusion White significantly superior powdery mildew resistance compared to standard large-flowered Z. elegans varieties — a critical practical advantage in zone 9's summer humidity where mildew routinely disfigures and eventually kills non-resistant zinnia varieties. The pure white flowers provide an exceptionally versatile garden color — combining harmoniously with virtually every other color, useful as a neutral presence that separates vivid hues without disappearing into the background, and reflecting light effectively in evening gardens when white flowers glow at dusk. The continuous flowering without deadheading and the compact, spreading habit (rather than the tall, upright habit of large-flowered types) make Profusion White an excellent mass-planting zinnia for zone 9 borders, containers, and butterfly gardens where the prolific white flowers attract skippers, sulphurs, and painted ladies throughout the summer.
sources: All-America Selections — Zinnia Profusion White Award
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Interspecific Zinnia Hybrids for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Summer Annuals for Gulf South Flower Gardens
